claim: For all integers n with absolute value greater than 2, Euler's totient phi(|n|) is even.
If n is a prime greater than 2, then n-1 is even.
For all n > 2, the number of integers k in [1, n-1] coprime to n is even.
All primes greater than 2 are odd integers.
The sum of divisors sigma(p) for an odd prime p is even.
For any odd prime p, the sum phi(p) + sigma(p) is a multiple of 4.
For any odd prime p, the average of phi(p) and sigma(p) is the prime p itself.
If n is prime and n > 2, then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 8.
For all integers n, n^3 - n is divisible by 3.
If n is prime and n > 3, then n^2 - 1 is divisible by 24.
If n is a power of 2 greater than 2, phi(n) is a power of 2.
If p is an odd prime, p divides 2^(p-1) - 1.
If p is an odd prime, 2^phi(p) is congruent to 1 modulo p.
If p is prime, the number of divisors tau(p) is 2.
For any prime p, sigma(p) + phi(p) = p * tau(p).
If phi(n) is a power of 2, then the odd prime factors of n are Fermat primes.
predicate: (phi(abs(n)) == 0 or (phi(abs(n)) & (phi(abs(n))-1)) != 0) or all(not is_prime(p) or p == 2 or any(p == 2**(2**k)+1 for k in range(5)) for p in range(3, abs(n)+1) if abs(n)%p == 0)

claim: If n is the product of two distinct odd primes, phi(n) is divisible by 4.
If n = pq for distinct odd primes p, q, then sigma(n) is divisible by 4.
If n is a product of k distinct odd primes, phi(n) is divisible by 2^k.
predicate: (abs(n) % 2 == 0) or (phi(abs(n)) % pow(2, len([p for p in range(2, abs(n)+1) if is_prime(p) and abs(n)%p == 0])) == 0)

claim: The number of integers k < n such that gcd(k, n) = 1 is even for n > 2.
If gcd(k, n) = 1, then gcd(n-k, n) = 1.
predicate: all(gcd(k, abs(n)) != 1 or gcd(abs(n)-k, abs(n)) == 1 for k in range(1, abs(n)))

claim: For n > 2, the sum of integers less than n and coprime to n is n * phi(n) / 2.
predicate: (abs(n) <= 2) or (sum(k for k in range(1, abs(n)) if gcd(k, abs(n)) == 1) * 2 == abs(n) * phi(abs(n)))

claim: For n > 2, the sum of integers coprime to n and less than n is a multiple of n.
predicate: (abs(n) <= 2) or (sum(k for k in range(1, abs(n)) if gcd(k, abs(n)) == 1) % abs(n) == 0)

claim: The sum of divisors of a perfect square n^2 is odd.
The sum of divisors sigma(n) is odd if and only if n is a square or twice a square.
For all n > 2, if sigma(n) is odd, then n must be a perfect square.
For prime p, phi(p^k) = p^k - p^(k-1).
predicate: (not is_prime(abs(n))) or all(phi(abs(n)**k) == abs(n)**k - abs(n)**(k-1) for k in range(1, 4))

claim: For any prime p and k >= 1, phi(p^k) is even unless p=2 and k=1.
predicate: (not is_prime(abs(n))) or all(abs(n)**k == 2 or phi(abs(n)**k) % 2 == 0 for k in range(1, 4))

claim: If n is prime and n > 3, then n is congruent to 1 or 5 modulo 6.
If p > 3 is prime, then sigma(p) is divisible by 6 if p is 5 mod 6.
If n > 1 and phi(n) is not divisible by 4, then n has at most one odd prime factor.

claim: For any odd prime p, p divides the binomial coefficient C(p, k) for 0 < k < p.

claim: The polynomial (1+x)^p is congruent to 1 + x^p modulo p for any prime p.
predicate: (not is_prime(abs(n))) or all((factorial(abs(n)) // (factorial(k) * factorial(abs(n)-k))) % abs(n) == 0 for k in range(1, abs(n)))

claim: Wilson's theorem: (p-1)! is congruent to -1 modulo p.
If n is composite and n > 4, (n-1)! is divisible by n.
For n > 4, n is prime if and only if gcd(n, (n-1)!) = 1.
For any odd prime p, the product of all integers k < p is congruent to p-1 mod p.

claim: For all n > 1, phi(n) is at most n - sqrt(n) for composite n.
For any prime p, the smallest value of phi(n) for n having p as its smallest prime factor is n(1 - 1/p).

For all integers n > 2, n divides phi(n) * sigma(n) + 1 if and only if n is prime.
For prime p, the product phi(p) * sigma(p) is p^2 - 1.
For any odd prime p, p^2 divides phi(p^2) + p.
For any prime p > 2, phi(p^2) is a multiple of p.
If n is a perfect square n=k^2 and k > 2, phi(n) is a multiple of the square root k.
For prime p, sigma(p) and phi(p) cannot both be perfect squares for p > 3.

The order of any element in the multiplicative group of integers modulo n divides phi(n).
